authors_affiliation: Zhaowei Zhu, Han Wang et al., Ping An Technology (Beijing, China) and National University of Singapore / National University Health System (Singapore)

result.evaluation_split: official hidden test set ("full test")

self_reported_metrics.other: Challenge validation score 0.682 (online validation set, distinct from the 0.514 full-test/hidden score). Offline evaluation (Table 1) of the final ensembled+rule model: 20% held-out offline test 0.683, external Hefei dataset test 0.319, full training set 0.885

model.architecture_details: Ensemble of two SE-ResNet (Squeeze-and-Excitation ResNet) models — one trained on 10-second input segments, one on 30-second segments — each with residual blocks (channel progression roughly 64->128->256->512) incorporating SE blocks for adaptive channel-wise feature reweighting. Combined with a rule-based bradycardia classifier (Pan-Tompkins R-peak detection on lead I to compute R-R intervals) whose negative predictions override the deep model, and an SNR (sinus rhythm) postprocessing step that assigns the default normal class when all class predictions are negative.
model.key_novelty: Ensembles SE-ResNet models operating on different input segment lengths (10s and 30s) trained with a custom multi-label 'sign loss' that down-weights easy/true-negative-dominated loss contributions, combined with a clinically derived rule-based bradycardia override to correct a class the deep model handled poorly.

input.leads_used: 8 (lead III, aVR, aVL, and aVF excluded from training data since they are linearly derivable from other leads via Einthoven's Law and Goldberger's equations); lead I additionally used standalone for Pan-Tompkins R-peak/R-R interval detection in the rule-based bradycardia model
input.signal_length_window: Two window lengths used and ensembled: 10-second and 30-second segments (longer signals truncated, shorter signals zero-padded)
input.sampling_rate: 500 Hz (original data ranged 257-1000 Hz across source datasets; PTB downsampled from 1000 Hz to 500 Hz to unify sampling rate; INCART dataset excluded entirely)
input.preprocessing: Biorthogonal wavelet denoising (2 and 6 vanishing moments for decomposition/reconstruction filters, refinement level 8); truncation/zero-padding to fixed 10s or 30s length; PTB resampled to 500 Hz

data.training_datasets: Official challenge training data from CPSC, CPSC2, PTB, PTB-XL, and Georgia datasets (INCART excluded); split into 30,172 training / 7,544 offline test examples; plus an external dataset from the Hefei Hi-tech Cup ECG Intelligent Competition (8-lead, 500 Hz, 10s recordings; 6,500 records with scored-class labels used as an external test set)
data.class_imbalance_handling: Custom multi-label 'sign loss' multiplying binary cross-entropy by a coefficient <1 for easily/correctly classified (true-negative-heavy) labels, emphasizing the loss contribution from misclassified/rare labels; CPSC dataset (originally labeled with only 9 classes) was relabeled by adding high-confidence predictions from a baseline model for classes among the 27 scored classes, validated by clinician review of a sample

training.loss_function: Custom multi-label 'sign loss' (binary cross-entropy weighted by a per-prediction sign coefficient)
training.optimizer: Adam; learning rate 0.001, rescheduled to 0.0001 at the 13th epoch
training.cross_validation_strategy: not reported (single 80/20 train/offline-test split, no k-fold cross-validation); optimal binarization threshold of 0.36 selected on the offline test set
training.hardware: Machine with 117 GB RAM, 4-core CPU, and one NVIDIA V100 GPU; trained for 19 epochs, batch size 16

contribution.summary: Ensembles two SE-ResNet models operating on 10s and 30s ECG segments, trained with a custom class-imbalance-aware 'sign loss', and combines them with a clinical rule-based bradycardia corrector; also relabels the CPSC dataset's missing labels and validates on an external Hefei dataset to improve generalizability.

authors_affiliation: Hosein Hasani, Adeleh Bitarafan, Mahdieh Soleymani Baghshah; Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

result.evaluation_split: official hidden test set (full test score 0.437, out of 41 teams in the final official ranking); paper also reports an official validation score of 0.609 and 5-fold cross-validation on the full training dataset (0.629±0.003)

self_reported_metrics.other: Table 1 metrics ('Ours') are measured on a 10% hold-out validation split of the training data: AUPRC 0.96, Fβ 0.61, Gβ 0.37, Challenge score 0.64. Official Challenge scores: validation 0.609, full test 0.437, 5-fold CV on full training set 0.629±0.003. Leave-one-domain-out (CPSC/CPSC-Extra/PTB-XL/G12EC) evaluation: 0.352 with adversarial domain generalization vs. 0.343 without.

model.architecture_details: Two parallel 1D CNN branches — one with larger convolution kernels for coarse features, one with smaller kernels for fine features — each followed by max pooling and a bidirectional LSTM (128 units). The concatenated feature vectors plus age/sex attributes feed (1) a gradient-reversal layer into a 2-layer MLP domain classifier (4 domains: CPSC, CPSC-Extra, PTB-XL, G12EC) for adversarial domain generalization, and (2) 27 separate binary sigmoid classifiers (one per scored class), thresholded via grid search maximizing the challenge score.
model.key_novelty: Adversarial multi-source domain generalization: a gradient-reversal domain classifier is trained jointly with the CNN-BiLSTM feature extractor to make learned features invariant across the different source ECG databases (treated as distinct 'domains'), combined with a custom class-weighted multi-label loss derived from the challenge's own reward/misclassification-penalty matrix.

input.signal_length_window: Fixed-length input window; shorter signals randomly padded and longer signals randomly cropped during training (with occasional additional cropping/padding to vary effective length as an augmentation)
input.preprocessing: Resampled to 250 Hz and normalized by a constant scale; records without any of the 27 scored labels excluded from training; no explicit denoising filter applied — instead noise addition/removal is used as a data augmentation technique to increase robustness

data.training_datasets: Official Challenge 2020 training data; for the domain-generalization experiment, CPSC, CPSC-Extra, PTB-XL (10k-sample subset used to avoid dataset dominance), and G12EC were treated as four separate domains in a leave-one-domain-out evaluation; 5% of training data held out for validation (half for threshold tuning, half for model selection)
data.class_imbalance_handling: Custom class-weighted binary cross-entropy loss using an importance/weight matrix derived from the challenge's reward matrix (Mw = 1 − W_reward + α·I); for multi-label records, the element-wise minimum across the corresponding rows of the weight matrix is used
data.augmentation_techniques: Random ECG padding/cropping; randomly adding or filtering low- and high-frequency noise components; low-probability random substitution of a lead's signal with zeros or low-frequency noise; random shuffling of lead order; random inversion of one or more leads; random band-pass filtering; random amplitude scaling or offset

training.loss_function: Per-class weighted binary cross-entropy across 27 binary classifiers (weights derived from the challenge reward matrix), summed with an adversarial domain-classification cross-entropy loss from a gradient-reversal layer (total loss L = L_C + λ·L_D)
training.optimizer: not reported
training.cross_validation_strategy: 5-fold cross-validation on the full training dataset; additionally, leave-one-domain-out evaluation across CPSC, CPSC-Extra, PTB-XL, and G12EC treated as 4 domains

contribution.summary: Combines a dual-branch (fine/coarse-kernel) CNN-BiLSTM feature extractor with adversarial multi-source domain generalization and extensive data augmentation, treating each source database as a distinct domain to improve generalization to the unseen test set; placed 5th of 41 teams in the official ranking.

result.evaluation_split: official hidden full test set (0.420 challenge score, 6th place); also 10-fold cross-validation on training data (0.695 challenge score for the final WRN-18-2 model)

self_reported_metrics.other: 10-fold CV ablation results for final WRN-18-2 model: F2 = 0.696, G2 = 0.448, challenge score = 0.695. Per-database challenge scores on the full hidden test set (Table 3): DB1 = 0.688, DB2 = 0.654, DB3 = 0.228.

model.architecture_details: 18-layer Wide Residual Network (WRN-18-2): an input stem, four stages of N residual blocks (widening factor k=2), and an output stem. Each residual block uses two pre-activation convolutions with batch normalization, ReLU, and dropout between the convolutions, with 2x down-sampling at the first layer of each stage. Variable-length recordings are handled via a sliding window (4,096 samples, ~4s, with 512-sample/~1s overlap): each window is fed through the model up to the max-pooling layer, intermediate outputs are concatenated and max-pooled into a fixed-length vector, then passed through a final dense layer with sigmoid activation for multi-label output. Final model is an ensemble of 10 WRN-18-2 models from 10-fold cross-validation.
model.key_novelty: Introduces a 'bag of tricks' layered onto a wide ResNet backbone: a confusion-weighted binary cross-entropy (CoW-BCE) loss derived directly from the Challenge's own reward/confusion matrix, a warmup + cosine-annealing learning-rate schedule, Manifold Mixup applied to hidden representations (to handle variable-length recordings), class-specific decision thresholds, and a 10-model ensemble — each shown empirically to incrementally improve the official challenge score.

input.signal_length_window: Variable-length recordings processed via a sliding window of 4,096 samples (~4 seconds) with 512-sample (~1 second) overlap
input.sampling_rate: 500 Hz (recordings resampled to 500 Hz when necessary)
input.preprocessing: 50Hz and 60Hz notch filters to remove electrical noise; per-recording standardization (remove mean, scale to unit variance); resampling to 500 Hz where needed

data.training_datasets: All 6 official Challenge 2020 training datasets (PTB-XL, Georgia, CPSC, CPSC-Extra, PTB, StPetersburg); PTB and StPetersburg excluded due to long average recording length, and recordings without any positive scored label removed, leaving 37,619 recordings split into 10 folds via iterative stratification; no external data used
data.class_imbalance_handling: Confusion-weighted binary cross-entropy (CoW-BCE) loss that down-weights the contribution of misdiagnoses between clinically similar classes, based on the Challenge's own reward matrix; iterative stratification used for the 10-fold split to preserve per-label positive-example distribution
data.augmentation_techniques: Manifold Mixup — linear interpolation of hidden (intermediate) representations and labels of pairs of training examples, with mixing coefficient λ ~ Beta(0.2, 0.2)

training.loss_function: Confusion-weighted binary cross-entropy (CoW-BCE), a modification of standard BCE that down-weights misdiagnoses with high reward-matrix similarity
training.optimizer: Adam; learning rate 0.001 with a warmup (10 epochs) + cosine-annealing schedule over 100 total training epochs; batch size 128; L2 weight decay 0.0005; dropout probability 0.3
training.cross_validation_strategy: 10-fold cross-validation with iterative stratification; final submitted model is an ensemble of the 10 fold-specific models, averaging both prediction scores and class-specific decision thresholds

contribution.summary: Systematically evaluates a collection of small refinements (filtering/scaling, an LR warmup+cosine schedule, a novel confusion-weighted BCE loss tied to the Challenge's reward matrix, Manifold Mixup, class-specific thresholds, and model ensembling) on top of a wide ResNet backbone, showing each refinement incrementally and cumulatively improves 12-lead ECG classification performance, reaching 0.695 challenge score in cross-validation and 0.420 (6th place) on the official hidden test set.

authors_affiliation: Max N Bos, Rutger R van de Leur et al., University Medical Center Utrecht, University of Amsterdam, Netherlands Heart Institute, and Central Military Hospital, The Netherlands

result.evaluation_split: official hidden test set

self_reported_metrics.other: 10-fold cross-validation on training data (before hidden test evaluation): AUROC 0.939 ± 0.004 and challenge metric 0.565 ± 0.005; challenge metric ranged 0.298-0.643 across the four hidden test set partitions; per-class AUROC ranged 0.751-0.992

model.architecture_details: Deep 1D exponentially dilated causal convolutional network (WaveNet/TCN-style, following Franceschi et al.) composed of 7 causal convolution blocks (dilation doubling from 1 to 64 across blocks), each block using causal convolutions (kernel size 3), weight normalization, leaky ReLU, and residual connections; channels go from 12 input leads to 108 (constant for the first 6 blocks) to 216 in the final block, yielding a 383-sample receptive field; followed by 1D adaptive max pooling to squeeze the temporal dimension and a 216-to-24 linear layer with sigmoid activation for multi-label output. Final model is an ensemble of 10 models (one per 10-fold CV split).
model.key_novelty: First use of exponentially dilated causal convolutions (TCN-style) for ECG classification, combined with transfer learning by pre-training on a large external physician-annotated dataset (254,044 ECGs from a different country/device manufacturer) and fine-tuning on the challenge data with the earliest convolutional blocks frozen.

input.signal_length_window: up to 10s (network supports variable length via causal convolutions + adaptive pooling); signals longer than 10s truncated to the first 10s, shorter signals zero-padded per training batch
input.preprocessing: Resampled to 500 Hz via linear interpolation; truncation to first 10 seconds for longer recordings; zero-padding per batch for shorter recordings

data.training_datasets: Official challenge training dataset (42,511 12-lead ECGs, mapped to 24 classes after combining 3 equivalent diagnosis pairs); plus an external pre-training dataset of 254,044 12-lead ECGs from University Medical Center Utrecht (UMCU), physician-annotated via a text-mining pipeline (with a held-out UMCU test set of 16,698 ECGs)
data.class_imbalance_handling: Weighted focal loss: positive-class weight set to the class imbalance ratio, scaled by a tunable weighting factor (0-1) between no weighting and full weighting, plus a focal loss gamma parameter (final model: gamma=2, weighting factor=0.5)

training.loss_function: Weighted focal loss (multi-label)
training.optimizer: Adam
training.cross_validation_strategy: 10-fold iterative stratification for multi-label data; best-performing model per fold selected and ensembled via mean of probability outputs; early stopping after 5 epochs without challenge-metric improvement
training.hardware: NVIDIA Titan Xp GPU (donated by NVIDIA Corporation); implemented in PyTorch 1.3, Python 3.7

contribution.summary: Combines transfer learning (pre-training on 254,044 ECGs from a different country and device manufacturer) with an exponentially dilated causal convolutional network, partially freezing pre-trained weights before fine-tuning on the challenge dataset, and ensembling across 10 cross-validation folds.

authors_affiliation: Najmeh Fayyazifar, Selam Ahderom, David Suter (Edith Cowan University, Perth); Andrew Maiorana (Curtin University; Fiona Stanley Hospital); Girish Dwivedi (Fiona Stanley Hospital; University of Western Australia), Perth, Australia

result.evaluation_split: official hidden test set (full test score); also reports Challenge validation-set score of 0.623 and 10-fold CV scores on training data

self_reported_metrics.other: 10-fold cross-validation scores on training data (using the same official Challenge metric): hand-designed CNN 59.2%, hand-designed Conv-BiLSTM (RCNN) 61.9%, Conv-BiLSTM with wavelet input 62.9%, NAS-based (DARTS) CNN with wavelet input 64.4% (this last model was not submitted/scored on validation or full test sets)

model.architecture_details: Two models proposed. (1) Hand-designed RCNN: 49 1D convolutional layers arranged in 16 blocks of 3 conv layers each (Conv-BN-ReLU-Dropout), with skip connections between blocks (ResNet-style concatenation), a stem conv layer with 32 filters, channel doubling at blocks 4/8/12 (up to 256 channels), filter length 24, followed by a BiLSTM layer with 300 hidden units and a final sigmoid layer with 24 output nodes. (2) NAS-based CNN: architecture discovered via Differentiable Architecture Search (DARTS) with a custom 1D search space (convolutions with filter lengths 3/5/7/9/11, dilated convolutions, max/average pooling, skip connections); final searched network stacked to 20 blocks (2 reduction blocks) with a sigmoid output layer.
model.key_novelty: Directly compares a hand-designed CNN+BiLSTM (RCNN) architecture against an automatically discovered CNN architecture (via DARTS neural architecture search) for the same 12-lead ECG multi-label classification task, to study the impact of architecture design choice versus automated search.

input.signal_length_window: 10s fixed window; signals shorter than 10s zero-padded, longer than 10s truncated to the last 10 seconds
input.preprocessing: Zero-padding/truncation to a fixed 10s window; for the NAS-based model, input was transformed via a 1-level Db1 wavelet decomposition, using only the approximation coefficients (details discarded) to reduce input size/compute

data.training_datasets: Official PhysioNet/CinC 2020 Challenge training data (43101 multi-label ECG samples originally; reduced to 37619 samples after discarding classes not among the 27 scored classes, collapsed to a 24-class task after merging 3 equivalent class pairs). No external datasets mentioned.
data.class_imbalance_handling: Used a moving/adjusted decision threshold (0.2 instead of the default 0.5) for the multi-label sigmoid outputs to account for class imbalance

training.loss_function: not reported
training.optimizer: RCNN model: Adam optimizer, He initialization, initial learning rate 1e-3 with ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler (decay 0.1, patience 3, min lr 1e-6), 50 epochs with early stopping (patience 15). NAS-based model: SGD with momentum 0.9, initial learning rate 0.025 with cosine annealing scheduler (min lr 1e-3); architecture search run for 50 epochs, final architecture trained from scratch for 200 epochs with early stopping (patience 20).
training.cross_validation_strategy: 10-fold cross-validation on the training dataset (random partition into 10 subsets; per fold: 8 for training, 1 for validation/early stopping, 1 held out for testing), mean of 10 folds reported
training.hardware: RCNN model trained on a single Nvidia GeForce GTX 1080 Ti GPU (~300s/epoch, up to 250 minutes total); NAS-based model search and training on a single Nvidia Quadro RTX 8000 GPU (~50 min/epoch for search; ~20 hours total training run time)

contribution.summary: Compares a hand-designed Conv-BiLSTM (RCNN) architecture against an automatically-designed CNN found via Differentiable Architecture Search (DARTS) for 12-lead ECG abnormality classification, finding the NAS-discovered architecture achieves higher cross-validation scores than the hand-designed model, though it was not scored on the official validation/test sets.

self_reported_metrics.f1: not reported as a single aggregate value; per-pathology F1 scores are reported in Table 1 for CPSC, CPSC2, PTB-XL, Georgia and the hidden validation set (e.g. AF: 0.9263/0.7394/0.9141/0.7528/0.83; STach: -/0.8305/0.8114/0.8865/0.844; LBBB: 0.7728/0.0506/0.8029/0.7432/0.697; full table in paper)

model.architecture_details: Not a neural network. A fully rule-based/feature-engineering pipeline: QRS detection via a combination of three independent detectors (phasor transform, continuous wavelet transform, S-transform), QRS morphology clustering by cross-correlation to isolate the dominant morphology, averaging of the representative PQRST segment, detection of P/QRS/T wave onsets and offsets (wavelet transform + thresholding using the bior 1.5 wavelet for QRS, phasor-transform-based detection for P waves, wavelet+tangent method for T wave offset), followed by hand-crafted feature extraction and per-pathology classification using medical-definition thresholds (for STach, SB, IAVB/LPR, LBBB, PR, NSIVCB, LQRSV, SNR) or classification trees (for AF, AFL, SA, RBBB, IRBBB, LAnFB, LAD, RAD, PAC, PVC, TAb).
model.key_novelty: An interpretable, fully rule-based (non-deep-learning) diagnostic pipeline that averages a representative PQRST complex per record and applies explicit medical-definition thresholds or shallow classification trees on hand-crafted morphological/interval features, prioritizing explainability over raw performance.

input.signal_length_window: Whole-record processing; only the QRS complexes of the dominant morphology cluster in each record are averaged into a single representative PQRST segment used for feature extraction (not a fixed-duration window)
input.preprocessing: QRS detection via combined phasor/continuous-wavelet/S-transform detectors; bandpass filtering (20-40 Hz) for AF-related feature extraction; QRS alignment and cross-correlation-based clustering by morphology; averaging of the dominant-morphology PQRST segment; wavelet-based (bior 1.5) onset/offset detection for QRS, phasor-transform-based P-wave detection, and wavelet+tangent-based T-wave offset detection

data.training_datasets: All 6 official Challenge databases used for evaluation/detection (CPSC2018, CPSC2/Southeast University China, St Petersburg INCART, PTB Diagnostic ECG Database, PTB-XL, Georgia); only CPSC, PTB-XL and Georgia databases were used to train the classification trees. No external (non-Challenge) data used.
data.class_imbalance_handling: not reported

training.loss_function: not applicable — rule-based/classification-tree method, not a trained loss-optimized neural network
training.optimizer: not applicable — classification trees used, not gradient-based optimization
training.cross_validation_strategy: 5-fold cross-validation used when fitting the classification trees (to avoid overfitting)

contribution.summary: Presents a fully interpretable, non-deep-learning ECG classification pipeline for 19 cardiac pathologies, based on QRS/P/T wave delineation, averaged representative PQRST morphology, and either medically-defined thresholds or shallow classification trees on hand-crafted features, trading some performance for transparency about which features and thresholds drive each diagnosis.

authors_affiliation: Charilaos Zisou, Andreas Sochopoulos, Konstantinos Kitsios — Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aristotle University of Thessaloniki, Thessaloniki, Greece

result.evaluation_split: official hidden test set; also report 0.593 via 5-fold cross-validation on the full training set

self_reported_metrics.other: 5-fold CV Challenge metric: CRNN 0.511, LightGBM 0.549, Ensemble 0.593; 5-fold CV AUROC: CRNN 0.908, LightGBM 0.935, Ensemble 0.946 (AUROC/Challenge metric values are on the training set via CV, not the hidden test set)

model.architecture_details: Ensemble of a 1D convolutional recurrent neural network (CRNN: convolutional layers with 24 filters each, LeakyReLU activation, batch normalization, dropout, a self-attention mechanism, and a bidirectional GRU) and LightGBM (gradient boosting decision tree using hand-engineered features: wavelet multiresolution analysis and wavelet-bispectrum higher-order statistics per lead, signal energy/autocorrelation/SNR/R-peak amplitude statistics, HRV time/frequency/nonlinear features, plus patient age and sex). The two models' class probabilities are combined via class-specific weighted averaging with class-specific thresholds, both tuned by a genetic algorithm (fitness = 5-fold Challenge metric average); final submission uses 10-fold bagging (10 ensemble models averaged).
model.key_novelty: Combines a deep CRNN with self-attention with a feature-engineered LightGBM model using class-specific weights and thresholds tuned by a genetic algorithm, yielding an interpretable per-class breakdown of which model type is more predictive (LightGBM favored for HRV-related rhythm classes, CRNN favored for QRS-morphology classes).

input.signal_length_window: 10s (truncated or zero-padded to 10s)
input.preprocessing: Data relabeling to 24 target scored classes (removing unscored diagnoses); 3rd-order low-pass Butterworth filter (20Hz cutoff) for high-frequency noise; Notch filter (0.01Hz cutoff) for baseline wander; resampling to 500Hz and truncation/padding to 10s; per-lead normalization to zero mean/unit variance for CRNN input

data.training_datasets: All official challenge training datasets combined, relabeled from >100 raw classes down to 24 target scored classes (6 pairwise-identical classes merged, unscored diagnoses removed); no external data
data.class_imbalance_handling: SMOTE oversampling of the minority class to 10% of the majority class combined with random under-sampling of the majority class by 50%, applied for LightGBM's one-vs-rest binary classifiers

training.loss_function: LightGBM: binary logloss objective per one-vs-rest binary classifier; CRNN: not reported
training.optimizer: LightGBM: gradient boosting with learning rate 0.075, hyperparameters (feature fraction, lambda l1/l2, max depth, min child weight, min split gain, num leaves) tuned via Bayesian optimization, early stopping on validation AUROC; CRNN: Adam optimizer with Xavier weight initialization, early stopping on validation AUROC
training.cross_validation_strategy: 5-fold iterative-stratification cross-validation for multi-label data (per Sechidis et al.); genetic algorithm tunes ensemble weights/thresholds using 5-fold Challenge metric average as fitness; 10-fold bagging performed for the final submitted ensemble

contribution.summary: Proposes an ensemble of a CRNN (with self-attention and a bidirectional GRU) and a feature-engineered LightGBM model, combined via class-specific weights and thresholds optimized by a genetic algorithm, showing the two model types are complementary across abnormality types and outperform either alone.

authors_affiliation: Lucas Weber, Maksym Gaiduk, Wilhelm Daniel Scherz, Ralf Seepold; HTWG Konstanz, Germany, and I.M. Sechenov First Moscow State Medical University, Moscow, Russian Federation

result.evaluation_split: official hidden test set (full test score 0.27); paper also reports a validation score of 0.656 and per-subset hidden scores (Set 1: 0.840, Set 2: 0.300, Set 3: 0.190)

self_reported_metrics.other: 5-fold cross-validation on training data (not hidden test set): AUROC 0.943 (no augmentation) / 0.947 (with augmentation) / 0.970 (ensemble); AUPRC 0.567 / 0.577 / 0.611; Challenge metric 0.602 / 0.611 / 0.642 (ensemble). The ensemble was not evaluated on the official hidden test set due to runtime restrictions.

model.architecture_details: Modified 1D residual CNN: an initial block of three convolutions (the latter two with skip connections), followed by D=3 repeated groups of M=5 normal residual blocks (each pair of stride-1 convolutions plus a stride-3 bottleneck downsampling layer that doubles the feature count per repetition), then M additional normal blocks, a global max-pooling layer over each feature map (replacing intermediate pooling/batch norm), and a fully-connected classifier with one hidden layer that concatenates pooled convolutional features with patient age and sex, ending in a 24-class sigmoid output. ELU activations throughout; convolution kernel width W=5.
model.key_novelty: Removes intermediate pooling layers and batch normalization in favor of a single global max-pooling layer after the convolutional feature extractor, making the network fully independent of input signal length so it can train and predict on ECG segments of arbitrary duration, combined with five custom raw-signal data-augmentation methods (slicing, CutOut, channel shifting, additive noise, dropout).

input.signal_length_window: Variable length; training uses random slices up to 10000 samples (20s), minimum 4000 samples (or full signal if shorter); testing uses the whole signal (predictions averaged across slices if the signal exceeds 10000 samples) — architecture is signal-length independent via global pooling
input.sampling_rate: 500 Hz (all signals resampled to 500 Hz via Fourier resampling from their original, varying sample rates)
input.preprocessing: Resampling to 500 Hz; division by each signal's gain value to obtain physical units; outlier removal via a 50-sample (0.1s) uniform-filter baseline, replacing samples deviating >2.5 mV from baseline with the signal mean, followed by a 5-sample (0.01s) median filter for smoothing; second-order Butterworth bandpass filter, cutoffs 0.5-45 Hz.

data.training_datasets: Full official PhysioNet/CinC2020 challenge training data (43101 signals); no external data reported; patient age and sex from the header (coded as -1 if missing) used as additional classifier inputs
data.class_imbalance_handling: not reported
data.augmentation_techniques: Signal slicing (variable-length random crops, up to 10000 samples); CutOut (per-lead masking with the signal mean, mask length 0 to 20% of channel length, applied with 35% probability per lead); channel shifting (circular shift, same amount for all channels up to 500 samples, or independently per channel up to 5 samples); additive white Gaussian noise (max amplitude 20 microvolts); Dropout (50%) in the fully-connected classifier.

training.loss_function: Binary cross-entropy
training.optimizer: Adam, learning rate 1e-4, weight decay 1e-5
training.cross_validation_strategy: 5-fold cross-validation on training data for augmentation/ensemble comparisons; final submitted single model trained on a 90%/10% train/validation split; decision threshold of 0.071 (applied uniformly across classes) chosen by maximizing the challenge metric on the validation set
training.hardware: Tesla K40 GPU (Amazon Web Services); PyTorch framework; approximately 12 hours training time per model

contribution.summary: Adapts a 1D residual CNN with a single global max-pooling layer to make the network invariant to variable ECG signal length, and shows that five lightweight raw-signal data-augmentation techniques (slicing, CutOut, channel shift, additive noise, dropout) improve cross-validated generalization performance over the same architecture trained without augmentation.

model.architecture_details: Per-class binary classifiers trained on hand-crafted amplitude/timing feature maps derived from a single representative heartbeat per patient; boosting classifier (gradient-boosting style ensemble) was the best-performing model, ahead of random forest, SVM, logistic regression, k-NN, decision tree, and DL baselines (AlexNet-style CNN, LSTM).
model.key_novelty: A cache-inspired (least-recently-used) clustering procedure using structural similarity index (SSIM) to select a single representative 1-second heartbeat per patient, paired with a physiologically interpretable amplitude/timing feature generator, then classified per-arrhythmia with classical ML models (boosting won out over CNN/RNN/other ML models).

input.signal_length_window: 1-second representative heartbeat frame selected per patient (R-peak centered); for records >20s, multiple representations (up to 100) are sampled and voted over
input.sampling_rate: not reported (uses dataset-native sampling rate, denoted fs, in the segmentation algorithm)
input.preprocessing: 12th-order Butterworth low-pass filter (50Hz cutoff) plus channel-wise moving-average smoothing (10ms window); R-peak detection on Lead II via Pan-Tompkins (with a noisy-signal fallback method); heartbeats segmented into 1-second frames and clustered via an SSIM-based cache-like algorithm to pick one representative heartbeat per patient

data.training_datasets: 4 of the 6 official Challenge training sets used: Georgia, CPSC, CPSC_2, and PTB-XL 12-Lead ECG databases (PTB and St. Petersburg excluded due to sample size, length, and lower SNR); only records with at least one of the 27 scored classes were kept
data.class_imbalance_handling: Random undersampling: for the larger class in each binary classification task, samples were randomly subsampled to match the size of the smaller class

training.loss_function: not reported
training.optimizer: not reported
training.cross_validation_strategy: 80%/20% train/test split used for model and feature selection experiments per class; 5-fold cross-validation reported per dataset in Table 1

contribution.summary: Introduces a cache-inspired (LRU + SSIM similarity) method to distill each patient's 12-lead ECG into one representative heartbeat, plus a physiologically interpretable amplitude/timing feature-map generator, then shows a boosting classifier trained on these features outperforms both other classical ML models and basic deep learning models for multi-label arrhythmia classification.

model.key_novelty: Instead of classifying raw multi-lead signal, extracts a single subject-wise averaged cardiac-cycle 'template' from Lead II only (R-peak aligned via the Hamilton algorithm, resampled to 400 samples, split into two halves and swapped, amplitude-normalized), reducing each recording to one compact denoised waveform fed into a small CNN-LSTM classifier.

input.leads_used: Lead II only (of the 12 available leads)
input.signal_length_window: One averaged cardiac cycle per subject, resampled to a fixed 400-sample template (not a raw time window)
input.preprocessing: FIR bandpass filter, passband 3-45 Hz, applied to Lead II; R-peak detection via the Hamilton algorithm; signal segmented between R peaks; each cardiac cycle re-sampled to 400 samples and averaged per subject; resulting template split into two equal halves and swapped; amplitude-normalized to the template's maximum absolute value.

data.class_imbalance_handling: not reported

training.optimizer: Adam, learning rate 0.005
training.cross_validation_strategy: not reported — single split of available data into 64% training, 16% validation, 20% testing (not k-fold cross-validation); early stopping if loss did not decrease for 8 consecutive epochs (max 1000 epochs, batch size 256)

contribution.summary: Proposes a lightweight 'bio-toolkit' that reduces each 12-lead ECG to a single averaged Lead-II cardiac-cycle template and classifies it with a compact 2-layer CNN + 2-layer LSTM network with a per-class optimized decision threshold, aiming for a low-complexity model suitable for real-time use.

model.architecture_details: Twelve independent per-lead models, each a modified SqueezeNet CNN (transfer learning) fine-tuned on scalogram images generated from that lead's ECG signal via continuous wavelet transform; final six SqueezeNet layers modified (60% dropout, 1x1 conv with filters = number of output classes, classification layer without preset labels); predictions from the 12 lead-specific models combined via a voting scheme (each model contributes 0.083 per diagnosis code; codes with summed score >0.3 (>=4 votes) are assigned, else the max-scoring code(s) are used).
model.key_novelty: Trains a separate SqueezeNet transfer-learning CNN per ECG lead on wavelet scalogram images (rather than one model over all 12 leads), then fuses the 12 lead-level predictions with a simple vote-counting scheme to allow multi-label diagnosis with increased confidence.

input.signal_length_window: not reported

training.optimizer: Stochastic gradient descent with momentum (SGDM); initial learning rate 3e-4, mini-batch size 10, max epochs 15
training.cross_validation_strategy: not reported

contribution.summary: Applies wavelet-scalogram transfer learning with a modified SqueezeNet to build 12 separate per-lead ECG classifiers and combines their outputs with a vote-counting scheme, enabling multi-label arrhythmia diagnosis while leveraging lead-specific anatomical sensitivity to different arrhythmias.

model.architecture_details: 1D ResNet-style CNN feature extractor with 6 residual blocks (3 convolutional layers each, kernel size 3), local skip connections plus an additional global skip connection from the input layer, followed by Global Max Pooling and a final classification layer of 24 independent fully-connected binary classifiers (termed 'BUTT' - Binary Units Training Technique) with sigmoid outputs.
model.key_novelty: Extends a 1D ResNet with a global skip connection to the input, and derives a custom differentiable loss function directly from the official Challenge weighted-confusion-matrix metric, combined with per-class Bayesian-optimized decision thresholds.

input.signal_length_window: variable length; zero-padded to equal length for mini-batch training, with zero-padded portion cropped out immediately after the ResNet layers (before pooling) to avoid distorting pooled features; long Holter recordings split into segments at inference

data.training_datasets: 43,101 labeled recordings from the 6 official Challenge training databases; no external data reported
data.class_imbalance_handling: Weighted cross-entropy (WCE) loss with per-class positive/negative weights inversely proportional to class frequency
data.augmentation_techniques: Random ±10% stretch along the temporal axis (p=0.8) and ±20% amplification along the voltage axis (p=0.8); other tested preprocessing augmentations (baseline drift removal, temporal shifting, noise addition, simulated electrode switch) had no measurable effect.

training.loss_function: Two-phase training: weighted cross-entropy (WCE) in the first learning-rate cycle, then a custom differentiable 'CM Loss' derived from the official Challenge metric's weighted confusion matrix in the second cycle

model.architecture_details: Two-stage pipeline: (1) a UNet-like fully convolutional 1D network (4 down/upsampling levels with skip connections, 50% dropout in bottleneck layers, batchnorm + Mish activation) accepts variable-length 12-lead input and segments the ECG into P-wave/QRS-complex/T-wave regions while also classifying each QRS complex as normal, supraventricular, or ventricular extrasystole; (2) 54 hand-crafted features (timing intervals, wave amplitudes, Frank-lead vectorcardiographic angles, extrasystole proportions) derived from the segmentation output are classified with an XGBoost gradient-boosted-tree model (24 non-exclusive labels).
model.key_novelty: Uses a UNet segmentation network to produce human-interpretable intermediate ECG delineations (P/QRS/T boundaries and beat classification) and derives classical clinical features from these segments for downstream tree-based classification, prioritizing explainability over an end-to-end black-box classifier.

input.preprocessing: Resampled to 500Hz via FFT; bandpass filtered 0.05-42Hz; discrete wavelet transform (Daubechies Db4) with hard thresholding applied as a high-pass filter to remove residual baseline wander

data.class_imbalance_handling: not reported

contribution.summary: Proposes a two-step, explainable pipeline that first segments 12-lead ECGs into P/QRS/T waves and beat types with a UNet, then extracts classical clinical features from the segmentation for XGBoost classification, trading raw classification performance for interpretability.

model.architecture_details: 24 identical 1D-CNN sub-networks stacked in parallel, one per diagnosis class, each comprising 3 convolutional layers (kernel=11/stride=5/filters=64, kernel=5/stride=2/filters=64, kernel=3/stride=1/filters=1), each followed by ReLU, dropout (0.3), and batch normalization, then a flatten + dropout + dense layer with sigmoid activation producing a single scalar; the 24 per-class scalars are concatenated into a 24-dimensional output vector. model.key_novelty: Class-dependent threshold approach: instead of training separate multi-class models, each of the 24 parallel CNN branches is thresholded individually (threshold chosen from the ROC curve to maximize TPR-FPR), allowing multi-label/multi-class classification in a single inference pass.

model.architecture_details: ECG representation network: 1D convolutional layer + 16 residual blocks (2 conv layers each, filter width 16, filters starting at 32 and doubling every 4th block, stride-2 subsampling every 2nd block, BatchNorm + ReLU + dropout 0.2) followed by a bi-directional GRU layer to extract temporal features; a 3-layer Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) then models inter-label correlations, using a correlation matrix built from label co-occurrence conditional probabilities; final classification via dot product of ECG features and GCN label embeddings, trained with a proposed class-aware binary cross-entropy loss.
model.key_novelty: Combines a CNN+bi-GRU ECG feature extractor with a Graph Convolutional Network that explicitly models label co-occurrence/correlation structure (via a conditional-probability adjacency matrix) for multi-label classification, trained jointly with a novel class-aware BCE loss.

training.cross_validation_strategy: 5-fold cross-validation on the training set for ablation experiments

contribution.summary: Proposes an end-to-end multi-label ECG classifier that pairs a CNN+bi-GRU feature extractor with a Graph Convolutional Network encoding label co-occurrence correlations, trained via a new class-aware BCE loss, showing incremental gains from residual blocks, bi-GRU, and GCN in ablation.

model.architecture_details: Not a neural network: a two-level classical Random Forest classifier (max depth 20, sqrt max-features rule). Level 1 RF separates scored vs. unscored classes; Level 2 uses two separate RFs (one for scored classes with 400 features/600 trees, one for unscored classes with 200 features/200 trees), all trained on topological data analysis (TDA) features plus demographic data and RR-interval statistical moments.

input.leads_used: 12 (used in 7 different lead groupings per segment: one grouping spans all 12 leads, others represent clinically-referenced lead subsets covering all 12 leads collectively)

input.preprocessing: ECG viewed as multivariate time series; segmented into 3 portions (beginning/middle/end of 1800 time points each); converted into point-cloud embeddings per lead grouping (300 equally spaced time slices); persistent homology (via Ripser) computed on point clouds to derive topological features (barcode statistics, persistence landscapes, persistence entropy for dimensions 0 and 1); supplemented with demographic data and RR-interval statistical moments (Pan-Tompkins-based, via the Challenge-supplied toolbox)

model.architecture_details: Multi-stream network with two parallel branches concatenated by a final sigmoid-activated fully connected classification layer: (1) a feature stream — hand-crafted per-lead features (averaged beat, beat, atrial, rhythm, signal, QRS, and meta categories, 386 features total) fed into a 2-layer MLP (1024 nodes, dropout 0.08; then 128 nodes, dropout 0.1; leaky ReLU + batch normalization per layer); (2) a deep convolutional stream based on the 2018 CPSC-challenge winning model — 5 sequential 1D-convolutional blocks (each with three 1D-conv layers, 12 filters, kernel sizes 3/3/24 except kernel 48 in the final block, leaky ReLU, dropout 0.2), followed by a bidirectional GRU (20 nodes, dropout 0.2), an attention layer, and batch normalization. Separate models were trained per data source and combined via an ensemble with majority voting.
model.key_novelty: Concatenates a hand-engineered-feature MLP stream with a raw-signal deep CNN+BiGRU+attention stream into one jointly trained multi-stream model, and trains separate per-dataset-source models combined via ensemble majority voting to handle heterogeneous labeling pools across data sources.

model.architecture_details: Ensemble of four classifiers: three CNNs (P-CNN, QRS-CNN, T-CNN), each with one or more convolutional layers plus a fully connected layer, operating on concatenated multi-lead average P/QRS/T segments extracted from an averaged PQRST beat template, to classify morphology-altering abnormalities; a feed-forward neural network (Rhythm-NN, two hidden layers of 64 and 32 neurons) using RR-interval-derived features (median, std, min/max distance, RMSSD) for irregular-rhythm classes; and a threshold-based classifier on the vector-magnitude signal's maximum amplitude for PVC detection. Outputs of all sub-models are concatenated into the final label vector; "normal" is assigned only when no other abnormality is detected.
model.key_novelty: Combines classical average-template/segment-based ECG signal processing (per-lead averaged P, QRS, and T segments) with small dedicated CNNs per segment type and rhythm-only features, rather than feeding raw whole ECG signals into one large deep network.

input.signal_length_window: First 1-minute segment of each ECG (or less if shorter); average-beat templates built from R-260ms to R+370ms per lead (P segment R-260 to R-150ms, QRS R-50 to R+50ms, T R+100 to R+370ms)
input.preprocessing: Resampling to 500Hz; bandpass Butterworth filter (3rd order, zero-phase, 0.67-30Hz); beat detection via gqrs on vector magnitude, refined with the Woody algorithm; signal-quality check via average QRS cross-correlation (threshold 0.9), discarding 4752 low-quality signals; construction of per-lead average PQRST templates

training.cross_validation_strategy: Stratified 70/30 train/validation split per sub-model dataset; final submitted model retrained on all available data without splitting

contribution.summary: Proposes a hybrid ensemble that combines classical average-template ECG signal processing (per-lead P/QRS/T segment averaging and RR-interval features) with small dedicated neural networks per abnormality type, rather than training one large deep network on raw ECG signals.

model.architecture_details: EASTNet: a 34-layer 1D ResNet (modified from 2D image ResNet) with 1D Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks embedded in each residual basic block, followed by a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) classification head (512 -> 256 -> 24 nodes). First convolution kernel enlarged to size 15 to increase receptive field, followed by a 7x7 convolution; max pooling replaced with a strided convolutional layer; dropout layers added for robustness.
model.key_novelty: Combines a heartbeat-level random sampling data augmentation strategy (concatenating several randomly selected single-heartbeat segments as one input) with a 1D SE-ResNet feature encoder whose SE blocks let the network learn inter-lead channel importance.

data.class_imbalance_handling: Statistics collected per abnormality class, then selective discarding and duplication of records to balance the class distribution as much as possible
data.augmentation_techniques: Random combination of intercepted single-heartbeat segments (compared 1-6 beats, both continuous and random sampling; 4 random beats chosen as final input) used explicitly as a data augmentation method that reduces overfitting

training.loss_function: not reported

model.architecture_details: Lightweight 1-D ResNet-18-based backbone (12 leads treated as input channels) with two parallel convolutional branches of the same kernel size (3) but different dilation rates (1 and 2) for multi-scale feature extraction, instance normalization instead of batch normalization, and anti-aliased downsampling blocks (max-pool with stride 1 followed by a blur-pooling layer with stride 2, per Zhang 2019) for shift invariance. Age and sex are concatenated with the pooled feature vector before two fully-connected layers and a combined sigmoid/softmax output layer. Input records are split into fixed-length segments processed independently, with segment-level probabilities averaged (soft voting) to produce a record-level prediction.
model.key_novelty: Combines anti-aliased (blur-pooled) downsampling blocks with a very low 80 Hz sampling rate to build a lightweight, real-time-capable ECG classifier, and randomly alternates between weighted cross-entropy (softmax) and Lovász-softmax (sigmoid) losses during training to encourage inter-class competition in the multi-label setting.

input.signal_length_window: Variable-length records split into fixed-size segments (segment length n samples; per-segment predictions averaged for the record-level output)
input.sampling_rate: 80 Hz (downsampled; authors compared 60, 80, and 100 Hz and selected 80 Hz to balance information content vs. lightweight computation)
input.preprocessing: Downsampling to 80 Hz; per-lead mean subtraction; exclusion of records with signal absence or high noise (based on a standard-deviation threshold)

data.training_datasets: Pooled official Challenge training data from four sources: CPSC2018 (training + unused data), St Petersburg INCART 12-lead Arrhythmia Database, PTB (PTB Diagnostic ECG Database, 549 records, and PTB-XL, 21,837 records), and the Georgia database (Emory University). No external (non-Challenge) data used.
data.class_imbalance_handling: Undersampling: randomly removed 30% of normal-class records from the training set to address the long-tailed class distribution (Normal class dominant)
data.augmentation_techniques: Gaussian noise addition, random erasing, and random shifting

training.loss_function: Randomly alternates per batch (via a uniform random variable v) between weighted cross-entropy loss (applied to softmax outputs) and Lovász-softmax loss (applied to sigmoid outputs) to encourage inter-class competition
training.optimizer: Adam optimizer, learning rate 0.0003, with gradient accumulation and back-propagation every 64 records; dropout probability 0.3
training.cross_validation_strategy: Simple 80/20 train/validation split of the pooled dataset (not k-fold cross-validation); challenge metric evaluated on the validation set every 3 epochs with best parameters saved

contribution.summary: Proposes a lightweight, real-time-capable 12-lead ECG classifier that runs on heavily downsampled (80 Hz) signals using a ResNet-18-based backbone with anti-aliased (blur-pool) downsampling and multi-scale dilated convolutions, plus a randomized dual-loss (cross-entropy/Lovász-softmax) training scheme and age/sex features.

model.architecture_details: 2-D convolutional neural network treating the 12-lead ECG as a 2D input with 'lead' and 'sample' dimensions; stacked Block1/Block2/Block3 residual-style convolutional blocks with decaying filter sizes and strides, Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) channel attention modules, batch normalization, spatial dropout, and a global average pooling layer feeding a fully-connected sigmoid output layer for 27-class multi-label prediction.

training.optimizer: Adam, initial learning rate 0.001, with early stopping and learning-rate reduction (factor 0.5) on plateau

input.preprocessing: Electrode-switch detection and polarity correction; signal-quality screening via bSQI index (examples with bSQI < 0.8 discarded, ~3213 excluded); Butterworth band-pass filter (0.13-88 Hz); two notch filters at 50 Hz and 60 Hz for power-line interference

data.class_imbalance_handling: Minority classes proportionally over-weighted in the one-vs-rest random forest classifiers; reduced problem to 24 multilabel classes by merging 3 pairs the challenge treats as equivalent (CRBBB/RBBB, PVC/VPB, PAC/SVPB)
data.augmentation_techniques: not reported (class imbalance addressed via external real-world databases and class re-weighting rather than synthetic augmentation)

contribution.summary: Shows that engineered digital biomarkers (HRV + 113 morphological features per lead) fed into a random forest outperform an end-to-end CNN-GRU deep learning model on this task, and that stacking both in an ensemble yields the best overall local score.

model.architecture_details: 8-layer CNN trained from scratch on 2-D continuous-Wavelet-transform scalogram images (200×1000×3 input) generated per heartbeat; architecture includes a 7×7 convolution layer, a normalization layer, ReLU activation, a pooling layer, a fully-connected layer (9 output neurons), and a softmax output; per-beat class probabilities are averaged across all beats in a recording to assign a primary diagnosis (and secondary diagnoses for classes averaging >10% probability).
model.key_novelty: Segments every beat in the 12-lead ECG, concatenates the same beat across all 12 leads into one signal, then converts it into a 2-D time-frequency image (scalogram) via continuous Wavelet transform (Morlet mother wavelet, 48 voices/octave) before CNN classification, enabling beat-to-beat primary and secondary diagnosis.

input.signal_length_window: Per-beat window centered on each detected R-peak, concatenated sequentially across all 12 leads to form one signal per beat
input.sampling_rate: Varies by source database (500 Hz for CPSC/PTB-XL/Georgia data, 257 Hz for St Petersburg INCART, 1 kHz for PTB); not resampled to a common rate as reported
input.preprocessing: Pan & Tompkins algorithm applied to raw lead I to detect R-peaks and compute mean RR interval, defining a time window per beat; window applied to remaining leads and beats concatenated across leads; resulting signal transformed into a 2-D wavelet scalogram via continuous Wavelet transform (CWT) with a Morlet mother wavelet and 48 voices per octave

data.training_datasets: All official 2020 Challenge training databases (CPSC 2018, St Petersburg INCART, PTB, PTB-XL, Georgia); 130,627 individual beats randomly extracted for training, covering 9 classes (only 9 of the 27 scored diagnoses were present in the initial-phase training data used)

model.architecture_details: Per-heartbeat feature vectors (discrete-time Fourier transform energy over 0-10Hz, inter-beat interval duration, plus static features: heart rate, IBI mean/std of differences, age, sex, signal gain) are projected via an embedding matrix and fed into a bidirectional LSTM (hidden dimension 256, input embedding dimension 512). The bidirectional LSTM outputs form attention vectors over the heartbeat embeddings; the resulting attention output feeds a final feed-forward layer with a sigmoid activation to produce multi-label probabilities across 24 classes.
model.key_novelty: Combines intra-heartbeat frequency-domain features (via discrete-time Fourier transform per beat) with an inter-heartbeat attention mechanism built on a bidirectional LSTM, allowing the model to weigh individual heartbeats' contribution to each diagnostic label.

input.leads_used: 12 (all 12 leads used by a custom heuristic to select the best-quality lead for R-peak/heart-rate detection)
input.signal_length_window: 10-second window extracted from the middle of each recording, with up to a maximum of 10 heartbeats processed per recording
input.preprocessing: All challenge datasets merged into one 43,101-recording set; 10s window taken from the signal middle; custom peak-detection pipeline selects the lead with the most prominent peaks via a gradient-ratio heuristic, estimates approximate heart rate via autocorrelation of the lead's derivative, then detects R-peaks using adaptive minimum-distance and prominence thresholds tuned to >99% accuracy on 50 sampled signals; per-heartbeat DTFT and static patient/signal features are then extracted

data.training_datasets: All official Challenge 2020 training datasets merged into one combined dataset (43,101 12-lead ECGs total); no external data reported
data.class_imbalance_handling: not reported

training.loss_function: Binary cross-entropy, averaged over all 24 class outputs
training.optimizer: not reported (the specific gradient optimizer algorithm is not named); learning rate, gradient clip norm, L1/L2 regularization coefficients, and dropout rate were tuned via Bayesian optimization using Hyperopt over 40 trials
training.cross_validation_strategy: Training data split 70%/30% into training and validation sets for hyperparameter tuning (40 Hyperopt trials); best-performing configuration then retrained on the full dataset before submission; classification thresholds per class tuned separately via 200 Bayesian-optimization trials on the training set

contribution.summary: Proposes representing each ECG as a sequence of per-heartbeat frequency-domain (DTFT) embeddings processed by a bidirectional LSTM with an inter-heartbeat attention mechanism, so the network learns which heartbeats matter most for each label; the submitted model generalized poorly to the hidden test set (score 0.024) and was not officially ranked due to submission omissions.

model.architecture_details: Benchmarks three state-of-the-art 1D CNN designs for time-series classification: InceptionTime (6 inception blocks, kernel sizes 40/20/10), ResNet (Wang et al. 1D CNN baseline, kernel size 5), and XResNet1d with 18/34/50/101 layers (kernel size 5). All share a common head: concat-pooling (concatenation of global average and max pooling) on top of the convolutional module, followed by FC-128 + batch norm + ReLU + dropout (p=0.5), then FC-27 output layer with sigmoid activation. Trainable parameter counts: 510491 (InceptionTime), 476187 (ResNet), 697499/1356955/1808795/3645339 (XResNet1d-18/34/50/101).
model.key_novelty: Systematically benchmarks InceptionTime, ResNet, and XResNet time-series CNN architectures with a shared decoding head on the 2020 Challenge's multicenter 12-lead ECG data, showing InceptionTime gives the best performance-to-parameter-count trade-off.

input.signal_length_window: Non-overlapping 2.5s chunks per recording (CNN input shape 12x250 at 100Hz); chunk-level probabilities averaged to obtain recording-level probabilities
input.preprocessing: Gain factor correction per lead; zero-phase 2nd-order band-pass Butterworth filter (0.5-40 Hz); downsampling to 100 Hz; per-lead standardization (z-score).

data.training_datasets: Subset of the official Challenge 2020 training data: CPSC + CPSC-Extra (6877+3453 examples), PTB-XL (21837 examples), and Georgia (10344 examples), merged into a 42511-example multicenter dataset; other official datasets excluded for not having comparable recording lengths. No external (non-Challenge) data used.
data.class_imbalance_handling: not reported (beyond use of stratified 5-fold cross-validation splits)
data.augmentation_techniques: Offline augmentation via non-overlapping 2.5s chunking of each 12-lead recording; online augmentation by randomly zeroing each lead signal with probability 0.5 during training, to improve robustness to recordings with zeroed-out leads.

training.loss_function: Cross-entropy
training.optimizer: Adam, learning rate 1e-3, batch size 32
training.cross_validation_strategy: 5-fold stratified cross-validation on the merged training dataset; trained up to 100 epochs with early stopping based on validation loss (20% of training set held out as validation)
training.hardware: NVIDIA Titan V GPU; PyTorch framework

contribution.summary: Benchmarks three state-of-the-art 1D CNN time-series architectures (InceptionTime, ResNet, XResNet) with a common classification head on multicenter 12-lead ECG data from the 2020 Challenge, finding InceptionTime offers the best trade-off between cross-validated performance and parameter count.

authors_affiliation: Sebastian D Goodfellow et al., Department of Critical Care Medicine, The Hospital for Sick Children / University of Toronto, Canada

result.evaluation_split: Official hidden test set: submission failed to run within the competition time limit, so the recorded score of -0.406 is a default/error score (equivalent to predicting all negatives), not a true measure of model performance. model.architecture_details: 15-channel input (12 ECG leads + 3 engineered r-peak/p-wave/t-wave likelihood feature channels). A stem of two 1D conv+batchnorm+ReLU+max-pool+dropout blocks downsamples the 19,000-sample input to 4,750 samples. This feeds a series of 8 non-causal WaveNet-style dilated residual blocks (dilation rates 1 to 256) with gated tanh/sigmoid activations and summed skip connections. Output conv layers (same structure as the stem) globally average-pool in time and feed a 27-unit dense layer with sigmoid activation, whose output is squared before thresholding. An auxiliary decoder branch (two upsampling deconvolution layers) reconstructs the original 19000x15 input as a secondary training objective.
model.key_novelty: Combines engineered ECG fiducial-point (r-peak/p-wave/t-wave) features as extra input channels with a modified non-causal WaveNet residual architecture, an auxiliary signal-reconstruction decoder, a squared-sigmoid output tuned to the competition's false-negative-sensitive metric, and post-hoc class-activation-map inspection to surface likely mislabeled training data.

input.signal_length_window: 19s fixed window (19,000 samples at 1000Hz), zero-padded if shorter, clipped after 19s if longer
input.sampling_rate: 1000 Hz (upsampled from original recordings sampled at 257, 500, or 1000 Hz)
input.preprocessing: Resampled to 1000Hz via SciPy resample; amplitude scaled by median R-peak amplitude on Lead I (via BioSPPy) when reliable r-peak detection was possible, otherwise per-lead median subtraction and division by the standard deviation across all 12 leads; 3 engineered feature channels (r-peak, p-wave, t-wave likelihood via kernel density estimation) appended as additional input channels

data.training_datasets: All official Challenge training data, no external data; used for 6-fold cross-validation
data.class_imbalance_handling: Sigmoid output squared to increase penalty on false negatives relative to false positives, aligned with the competition metric's sensitivity; no explicit class-reweighting scheme reported
data.augmentation_techniques: Synthetic noise added during training: Gaussian noise, high-frequency/low-amplitude oscillations, baseline wandering, and large-amplitude transient pulses; random amplitude scaling factor; random heart-rate perturbation with resampling (applied only when the label was not heart-rate dependent)

training.loss_function: Sum of binary cross-entropy (classifier output) and mean squared error (decoder reconstruction output)
training.optimizer: Adam, initial learning rate 1e-3, batch size 128, with ReduceLROnPlateau decay schedule (patience 1)
training.cross_validation_strategy: 6-fold cross-validation using the iterative-stratification package for multilabel stratified splitting; trained up to 100 epochs with early stopping (patience 10); inference used majority vote across the 6 fold models

contribution.summary: Combines raw 12-lead ECG with engineered r-peak/p-wave/t-wave likelihood features in a modified non-causal WaveNet residual network with an auxiliary reconstruction decoder, using class-activation maps to flag likely mislabeled training data; achieved a CV score of 0.63 but could not complete a valid test-set submission before the deadline.

Phase 2 (24 categories, all 6 databases, 10-fold CV): Challenge metric 0.030±0.009, AUPRC 0.083±0.001, F 0.116±0.004, Fβ 0.144±0.004, Gβ 0.062±0.002, Accuracy 0.148±0.009. Per-rhythm sensitivity/specificity values are given in Tables 1 (phase 1) and 2 (phase 2), e.g. phase 2 AF: Se 94.55%±1.19, Sp 93.97%±0.56.

model.architecture_details: Ensemble of classical machine-learning classifiers, each dedicated to a specific region of the cardiac conduction system: phase 1 used 4 classifiers (2 support vector machines for permanent conditions grouped by ECG region, plus 2 bagged-tree classifiers for the sporadic PAC/PVC events); phase 2 used 7 boosted-tree classifiers split by cardiac region (atrial tissue; sinoatrial node; AV node; bundle branches; ventricular tissue; plus dedicated PAC and PVC classifiers). Inputs are hand-crafted features: cross-correlation distances between intra-patient and rhythm-specific inter-patient morphological templates computed over defined ECG windows (P-wave, QRS complex, PQ-ST segment, T-wave, PR, RT), heart-rate-variability features (RR mean/SD, successive-difference SD, pNN50, RMSSD), Global Electric Heterogeneity (GEH) vectorcardiographic features, plus age and sex.
model.key_novelty: A physiologically-motivated feature-engineering approach: builds intra-patient and rhythm-specific inter-patient morphological templates for distinct ECG segments/cardiac regions and feeds the cross-correlation distances between them (plus HRV and GEH features) into region-specific classifiers, rather than an end-to-end deep network.

input.signal_length_window: Signals ranged from 6s to 60s (arrhythmias assumed present over the whole signal); fixed sub-windows anchored on the R-peak were extracted per segment of interest: P-wave (-300 to -40 ms), QRS-complex (-70 to +60 ms), PQ-ST (-150 to +250 ms, excluding -55 to +55 ms), T-wave (+100 to +350 ms), PR (-288 ms to R), RT (R to +258 ms)
input.preprocessing: Baseline removed via two median filters subtracted from the original signal; powerline/high-frequency noise removed with an equal-ripple FIR low-pass filter (3-dB point at 35 Hz); R-peaks detected via Pan-Tompkins algorithm on 5 positive-QRS leads (I, II, III, aVF, V4, V5, V6), combined into an impulse train, Gaussian-smoothed, cross-correlation aligned (V5 as reference), median-combined across leads, then Pan-Tompkins re-applied to the resulting signal

data.training_datasets: All six official Challenge 2020 databases (CPSC2018, St Petersburg INCART, PTB Diagnostic ECG Database, PTB-XL, Georgia 12-Lead ECG Challenge Database) — 43,101 labeled 12-lead ECGs; phase 1 evaluation restricted to the CPSC2018 subset only

training.optimizer: Not applicable — SVM and tree-based (bagged/boosted) classifiers
training.cross_validation_strategy: 10-fold cross-validation, implemented in Matlab 2020a, for both phase 1 and phase 2 classifiers

contribution.summary: Proposes a physiologically-interpretable ECG classifier that builds intra-patient and rhythm-specific inter-patient morphological templates per cardiac conduction region and feeds cross-correlation distances plus HRV/GEH features into an ensemble of region-specific SVM/tree classifiers, rather than using an end-to-end deep network.

model.architecture_details: Two parallel VGG-16-based convolutional branches trained from scratch: one branch takes 64x64 grayscale raw-signal time-series images, the other takes 64x64 grayscale time-frequency representation (TFR) images obtained via Wavelet Synchrosqueezing (WS). Each branch has 5 convolutional/max-pooling stages (3x3 kernels, 2x2 pooling), feeding into a 3-layer fully-connected head (4096-feature first FC layer) ending in a 27-channel sigmoid output layer.
model.key_novelty: Dual-branch (parallel) CNN that fuses raw ECG waveform images with wavelet-synchrosqueezing time-frequency images, feeding both representations into a shared VGG-16-derived architecture to capture complementary morphology and instantaneous-frequency features.

input.signal_length_window: 1.2 s segments taken from the first 6 s of each recording; 120 images generated per patient

training.cross_validation_strategy: 10-fold cross-validation; batch size and learning rate hyperparameters swept (batch sizes 64-1024, lr=0.001, 50 epochs), best batch size found to be 512

contribution.summary: Proposes a parallel dual-input CNN based on VGG-16 that combines raw ECG waveform images with wavelet-synchrosqueezing time-frequency images to classify 27 cardiac arrhythmia types from 12-lead ECGs, reporting only cross-validated results on the public training data.

authors_affiliation: Philip A. Warrick (PeriGen Inc., Montreal), Vincent Lostanlen (New York University), Michael Eickenberg (Flatiron Institute, New York), Joakim Andén (KTH Royal Institute of Technology), Masun Nabhan Homsi (Helmholtz Centre for Environmental Research, UFZ, Leipzig)

result.evaluation_split: 10-fold cross-validation on combined CPSC2018 + PTB-XL training data (90%/10% train/test split per fold, with 10% of training used for validation); authors explicitly state they were unable to complete a working Docker submission and therefore could not obtain a score on the official hidden test set

self_reported_metrics.other: Challenge metric (s_normalized, as defined in the Challenge overview paper) of 0.536 ± 0.012 (mean ± SD test-fold score over 10-fold cross-validation); training fold mean 0.569 ± 0.014, validation fold mean 0.540 ± 0.016. Authors caution this may be over-optimistic since it is not the official hidden test set score.

model.architecture_details: Three-stage differentiable pipeline: (1) a non-trainable time scattering transform (ST) per ECG channel using Morlet wavelets (Q=1, J=11, center frequency 200 Hz), producing 75 scattering paths (12 first-order + 63 second-order) with an asinh compressive nonlinearity, implemented in Python via the Kymatio library; (2) a depthwise separable convolution (DSC) layer (N=P=66, ReLU activation) combining scattering coefficients across the 12 leads; (3) two stacked bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM) layers (100 hidden units each) followed by a dense sigmoid output layer for multilabel classification of 24 target classes (27 arrhythmias with 3 equivalent-class pairs collapsed).
model.key_novelty: First fully Python-based implementation (via the new Kymatio library) of a scattering transform combined with a depthwise separable convolution and BiLSTM network for ECG arrhythmia classification, avoiding the MATLAB/Python split used in prior scattering-transform work.

input.signal_length_window: 30 s learning-batch window (ST paths for longer sequences truncated at 30 s; padding target applied for recordings shorter than the batch size)
input.sampling_rate: 500 Hz (ECG acquisition rate); scattering transform discretized at a Nyquist rate of 8 Hz
input.preprocessing: Analog-to-digital gain (ADG) from input headers applied to obtain physical (mV) units; channel-wise scattering transform with asinh compressive nonlinearity applied to scattering magnitudes

data.training_datasets: CPSC2018 (public CPSC training set + unused CPSC2 data) and PTB-XL, totaling 32,167 of the 43,101 available Challenge recordings (~75%); St. Petersburg and Georgia (G12EC) datasets were tried but training did not converge on them so they were excluded from cross-validation
data.class_imbalance_handling: not reported

training.loss_function: Binary cross-entropy (multilabel)
training.optimizer: Adam
training.cross_validation_strategy: 10-fold cross-validation; 90%/10% train/test split with 10% of the training set held out for validation and 20-epoch early stopping
training.hardware: Machine with 32 GB system memory and two GPUs (12 GB memory each), using TensorFlow's MirroredStrategy to distribute training across the two GPUs; ~18 hours training time per fold

contribution.summary: Combines a fixed (non-trainable) scattering transform with a learned depthwise separable convolution and bidirectional LSTM, all implemented end-to-end in Python via the Kymatio library, to classify 27 arrhythmia classes from 12-lead ECGs, but the team was unable to score on the official hidden test set due to submission/Docker issues.

self_reported_metrics.other: Aggregate Challenge statistics (not a per-team metric): 217 teams submitted 1395 algorithms; 707 successful entries overall (397 unofficial phase, 310 official phase); 70 teams' codebases fully evaluated on the test set; 41 teams qualified for the final rankings. High-performing algorithms exhibited performance drops of up to ~10% on hidden test data relative to validation; on average, scores dropped 47% from the hidden CPSC set to the hidden G12EC set, a further 57% from the hidden G12EC set to the hidden undisclosed set, and about 50% on average from the validation set to the test set. Median training time across submissions was 6 hours 49 minutes; over 70% of entries used hand-crafted/clinical features with classifiers such as SVMs, gradient boosting, random forests, and shallow neural networks rather than deep learning/CNNs.

contribution.summary: Defines the PhysioNet/Computing in Cardiology Challenge 2020 task: multi-label classification of 12-lead ECG recordings (66,361 recordings from six hospital systems in four countries; 43,101 posted publicly) into cardiac abnormalities drawn from 111 possible diagnoses, 27 of which were officially scored. Its central contribution is a novel evaluation metric — built from an expert (cardiologist)-defined reward matrix W that gives partial credit to misdiagnoses with similar clinical treatments/outcomes and full credit only to correct diagnoses — normalized so an always-correct classifier scores 1 and an always-inactive (all-normal) classifier scores 0, replacing plain accuracy/AUC as the ranking criterion. The Challenge also required, for the first time in a public competition of this kind, that teams submit open-source code for both training and running their models to ensure reproducibility.
contribution.code_url: https://physionetchallenges.github.io/2020/ ; scoring/evaluation code and official results at https://github.com/physionetchallenges/evaluation-2020

contribution.summary: This is the 4-page CinC2020 conference companion paper (DOI 10.22489/CinC.2020.236) to the PhysioNet/Computing in Cardiology Challenge 2020 overview, written by the challenge organizers rather than a competing team. It defines the task of multi-label classification of 12-lead ECG recordings into cardiac abnormalities (66,361 recordings sourced from five hospital systems in four countries; 43,101 shared publicly; 111 total diagnoses, 27 of which were officially scored) and introduces the Challenge's novel scoring metric: a cardiologist-defined reward matrix that gives partial credit to misdiagnoses with clinically similar treatments/outcomes, normalized so an always-correct classifier scores 1 and an always-inactive classifier scores 0. Reports that 217 teams submitted 1395 algorithms, 41 qualified for final ranking, and lists the official top-10 leaderboard.

It describes a system built on the PhysioNet/CinC 2018 Challenge dataset (apnoea/non-apnoea arousal detection from polysomnogram signals), merely published in the same CinC 2020 proceedings volume (DOI 10.22489/CinC.2020.340).
authors_affiliation: Philip de Chazal, John Du, Nadi Sadr; Charles Perkins Centre and School of Biomedical Engineering, The University of Sydney, Australia

result.evaluation_split: 10-fold cross-validation on the 2018 PhysioNet/CinC Challenge PSG dataset (994 overnight recordings); this is unrelated to the 2020 12-lead-ECG Challenge's official hidden test set / official weighted-accuracy metric.

self_reported_metrics.other: Two-class (arousal vs. non-arousal) results: AUROC=0.91, AUPRC=0.74, optimal specificity=sensitivity=0.85. Three-class (non-arousal / non-apnoea arousal / apnoea arousal) results: volume under ROC surface (VUROS)=0.75, non-arousal specificity=0.72, apnoea-arousal sensitivity=0.76, non-apnoea-arousal sensitivity=0.69, AUPRC=0.18 (non-apnoea arousal) and 0.76 (apnoea arousal). LDA baseline comparison: VUROS=0.68, two-class AUROC=0.88.

model.architecture_details: Bank of 10 single-hidden-layer feed-forward neural networks (20 hidden units each, softmax output over 3 classes), with outputs combined by averaging the ten classifiers' probability estimates; compared against a linear discriminant analysis (LDA) classifier baseline. A first-order-hold upsampler converts epoch-level classifications to a 200Hz output annotation.
model.key_novelty: Extends the authors' prior 2018 Challenge hand-crafted-feature FFNN system (which only detected non-apnoea arousals) to jointly classify both apnoea-related and non-apnoea-related arousals (3-class) from multi-signal polysomnogram data, increasing clinical applicability.

input.leads_used: not applicable — uses polysomnogram (PSG) channels (SpO2, chin EMG, EOG, airflow, abdominal/chest respiratory effort, 6 EEG derivations: F3-M2, F4-M1, O1-M2, O2-M1, C4-M1, C3-M2), not 12-lead ECG
input.signal_length_window: 15-second epochs; multi-epoch context formed by combining features from 4 epochs on either side of the current epoch (~1 minute look-ahead/look-back)
input.preprocessing: Artefact filtering and signal normalization per channel prior to epoch-based feature extraction; 59 time- and frequency-domain features extracted per 15s epoch (e.g. kurtosis, skewness, relative band energy, approximate entropy, signal envelope coefficient of variation).

data.training_datasets: 2018 PhysioNet/Computing in Cardiology Challenge PSG dataset (994 overnight recordings from Massachusetts General Hospital sleep laboratories); no 2020 Challenge 12-lead ECG data used.
data.class_imbalance_handling: not reported

training.loss_function: Cross-entropy cost function (for the FFNN classifier)
training.optimizer: not reported
training.cross_validation_strategy: 10-fold cross-validation: 994 records randomly divided into ten sets of 99-100 records, nine used for training and one for testing, rotated across folds

contribution.summary: Out of scope for the 2020 12-lead ECG classification challenge benchmark: this paper presents an extension of the authors' 2018 Challenge polysomnogram-arousal-detection system, using a bank of feed-forward neural networks to jointly detect apnoea and non-apnoea arousals from PSG signals.
